critique.summary: The article is a farewell message from the Sora app team announcing the discontinuation of the Sora app and thanking its community for their contributions. They also mention that more information about timelines and data preservation will be shared soon.
mainPoints: The team thanks creators and the community for their involvement.
Further details about the app's shutdown timeline and how to preserve user work will be provided.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with the official communication from the Sora app's verified social media account. The announcement is straightforward and does not contain unverifiable or exaggerated claims. However, since this is a direct statement from the company, it is accurate as a primary source but does not provide external perspectives or additional context.
considerations: The article does not provide reasons for the discontinuation of the app.
There is no information on alternatives or migration options for users.
The impact on the user community and any support plans post-shutdown are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on OpenAI's decision to shut down Sora, an AI video generation app. Commenters debate its lack of lasting appeal, questionable business strategy, and challenges competing with other AI companies, while some express skepticism about its consumer value and speculate on OpenAI's future focus on coding and enterprise markets.

comments.author: meken
comments.text:
I had so much fun making videos with my mom when it came out. During the first two weeks, we made over 100 cameo videos together - we were constantly running up against the upload limit. It unleashed tons of genuine creativity, joy, and laughter from us.

After those first two weeks though, we just… didn’t use it again. The novelty wore off and there wasn’t anything really to bring us back. That was the real downfall of Sora.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced user perspective highlighting initial excitement but eventual loss of interest, which aligns with common patterns in consumer app adoption and abandonment.

comments.author: password54321
comments.text:
"OpenAI’s top executives are finalizing plans for a major strategy shift to refocus the company around coding and business users" - WSJ

Coding is where the money is. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46432791#46434072
comments.analysis: This comment references a credible external source (WSJ) and accurately summarizes OpenAI's strategic pivot, which is consistent with industry reports.

comments.author: ex-aws-dude
comments.text:
The thing that didn't make sense with this app: who would ever want to scroll only AI generated videos over a combined feed?

In practice people would just generate the videos with the app then post them on regular social media in which case OAI would not get the ad revenue for that

Its the age-old "your product is just a subset of another product"
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully critiques the app's product-market fit, noting the impracticality of a standalone AI video feed compared to integrated social media platforms.

comments.author: mikhmha
comments.text:
I tried using Sora for a month. Never paid for it. I tried many different ways of prompting and I was always underwhelmed by its output. The generation would also take so long and there was like a 50% chance it would fail due to content violations. I will say though that it was kind of addicting in a way. Just trying to crank the lever and see what would come out. But you'd always leave disappointed. It was a casino where the operator was losing money for every play.

I think OpenAI had a brief delusion that it could become some huge social networking app. The App was heavily modeled after TikTok..
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed user experience with technical limitations and content moderation issues, supporting the view that Sora had usability and reliability problems.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: Not good, seems like they are running out of cash and partners abandoning them. They had no real moat to be fair. Anthropic eating their lunch in enterprise and other players have cashflows from other businesses (XAI, Google)
comments.analysis: Offers a plausible business analysis of OpenAI's challenges including financial pressures and competition, though some claims about cash flow are speculative.

comments.author: vermilingua
comments.text: Good riddence to bad trash. To me, this idea represents the absolute worst of the AI wave (out of a lot to choose from): a corporate controlled endless stream of the feelies to keep people plugged in and scrolling for nobody’s benefit except those in control of the output. If “entertainment” can be produced algorithmically to a volume and level of quality that the masses find attractive, it’s only a matter of time before bad (worse?) actors take control of it to start highly targeted campaigns of influence, far worse than what we’ve already seen.
comments.analysis: Expresses a critical and emotional viewpoint on AI-generated content's societal impact, raising valid concerns about manipulation but with a strong negative bias.

comments.author: foolfoolz
comments.text:
as a sora user:

- sora was not great at making what you asked

- i probably got 3 good videos out of 100 gens

- every video that was good needed editing outside of sora (and therefore could not be shared within sora)

just my experience
comments.analysis: Shares a candid user perspective on the app's limitations and the need for external editing, reinforcing the idea that Sora's output quality was insufficient.

comments.author: rfarley04
comments.text: It's just the social app being killed off, no? Wouldn't this line up with rumors that they'll soon let you create videos inside of chatgpt itself? I wish the actual video model would die but I assume this news is not that.
comments.analysis: Offers a reasonable speculation about product strategy, suggesting a shift from a standalone app to integration within ChatGPT, which fits known trends.

comments.author: bandrami
comments.text:
And this kills the Disney deal:

https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/business/digital/openai-sh...
comments.analysis: Links to a credible news source indicating that the shutdown impacts partnerships, providing factual context though without deeper analysis.

comments.author: strongpigeon
comments.text: I never quite got "why" they made it a separate app. While I'm sure it was fun for a while, this felt like something that had limited staying power as the novelty is what was driving it. People don't really want to switch between video apps for their entertainment and having it be Sora only is too limiting.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid product design critique about app fragmentation and novelty-driven engagement, which aligns with common challenges in consumer tech.
